summary: Add a new `swarmforge crap` subcommand that computes and reports CRAP (Change Risk Anti-Patterns) scores for Go functions. This is the enforcement tool for Constitution Rule 4 and will be wired into the Simplicity Enforcer agent in a later task. Your job is to convert every scenario in features/swarmforge-crap.feature into failing acceptance/unit tests so the Coder can drive implementation Red → Green → Refactor. DO NOT write production code — your deliverable is red tests only.

exit_codes.0: every row satisfies CC <= threshold-cc and CRAP < threshold-crap
exit_codes.1: at least one row violates either threshold
exit_codes.2: usage error, missing/unreadable coverprofile, or any I/O error

increments: +1 per `if` statement (each `if` counts, including `else if`; bare `else` does NOT count)
+1 per `for ... range` loop (same node type but distinct Gherkin bullet — still +1 each)
+1 per `case` clause in a `switch` (not the switch itself)
cc_rules.closure_folding: Any anonymous function (func literal) found inside a top-level function's body contributes its complexity upward. Only one row is emitted per top-level function. Closures do NOT get their own row.
cc_rules.scope: Top-level functions AND top-level methods (receiver methods on a type) each get a row. File-level init() functions also count as top-level.

coverage_rules.coverprofile_format: Standard Go coverprofile. First line may be `mode: set|count|atomic` and must be tolerated but NOT treated as a block. Block lines have the shape `<file>:<startLine>.<startCol>,<endLine>.<endCol> <numStmts> <count>`.
coverage_rules.intersection: A block belongs to a function iff the block's file path matches the function's file path AND the block's line range overlaps the function's line range (inclusive). Overlap semantics: block.startLine <= fn.endLine AND block.endLine >= fn.startLine.
coverage_rules.fraction: cov = sum(numStmts) for matching blocks where count > 0, divided by sum(numStmts) for all matching blocks. If the function has zero matching blocks, cov = 0.
coverage_rules.file_path_matching: Coverprofile records full package-qualified paths like `github.com/swarm-forge/swarm-forge/internal/crap/cc.go`. The Coder will need to resolve these against on-disk paths under --root. For the tests you are writing, use the coverprofile path verbatim in both fixture source filenames and block lines — do not require path resolution in the E2E tests unless a scenario explicitly calls for it.

output_rules.sort: CRAP descending, then CC descending as tiebreaker.
output_rules.coverage_display: Coverage shown as a percentage with one decimal, e.g. `40.0%`.
output_rules.crap_display: CRAP shown with two decimals, e.g. `12.48`.
output_rules.violations_display: A space- or comma-separated list (Coder's choice) from the set {"cc", "crap"}. Empty when the row satisfies both thresholds.
output_rules.json_schema: Array of objects with the fields: file (string), line (int), function (string), cc (int), coverage (float 0..1), crap (float), band (string: low|moderate|high), violations (array of strings, subset of ["cc", "crap"]).

in_scope_for_your_test_pass: features/swarmforge-crap.feature — ALREADY AUTHORED by the Architect. Do not modify; use as your source of truth. If you find a contradiction between a scenario and this handoff, flag it back to the Architect rather than silently reconciling.
internal/acceptance/swarmforge_crap_test.go — NEW. Acceptance-level tests that exercise the CLI surface: dispatch, flag parsing, exit codes, text/JSON rendering shape, self-application smoke test.

internal/crap/cc_test.go — NEW. Unit tests for cyclomatic complexity counting over in-memory Go source snippets (use go/parser + go/token + go/ast from stdlib). One table-driven test covering every CC rule in the Gherkin is preferred.

internal/crap/cover_test.go — NEW. Unit tests for coverprofile parsing and function/block intersection. Feed in literal strings representing coverprofiles; assert parsed blocks and computed coverage fractions.
internal/crap/analyzer_test.go — NEW. Unit tests for the top-level Analyze entry point: given a fake filesystem of Go sources and a coverprofile string, produce a []Row sorted correctly.
internal/crap/report_test.go — NEW. Unit tests for text rendering (column content, sort order, violations column) and JSON rendering (valid JSON, array, field names and types).
internal/cli/cli_test.go — UPDATE. Add TestDispatchCrap asserting that `crap` is dispatched to the new Config.Crap handler.

Optional: testdata fixtures under internal/crap/testdata/ — tiny Go files and coverprofiles with known CC/coverage. Prefer in-memory strings where practical to keep the test set self-contained, but testdata is acceptable when a file-walker needs real directory entries.
out_of_scope_for_your_pass: internal/crap/*.go (production code) — leave these absent or stub-only. The Coder will write them.

cmd/swarmforge/main.go — the Coder will add the crap handler wiring. You may reference the expected wiring in an acceptance test that exercises cli.Dispatch with a Config.Crap handler, but do NOT modify main.go.
go.mod — must stay on stdlib only. If you feel pulled toward a dependency (cobra, testify, etc.), DO NOT add it. Use the standard testing package.
features/swarmforge-crap.feature — already final for this pass.

suggested_types: type Row struct { File string; Line int; Function string; CC int; Coverage float64; CRAP float64; Band string; Violations []string }
type Thresholds struct { CC int; CRAP float64 }
type Config struct { Root string; Coverprofile string; JSON bool; Thresholds Thresholds; Stdout, Stderr io.Writer }
type Block struct { File string; StartLine, EndLine, NumStmts, Count int }
func CoverageForRange(blocks []Block, file string, startLine, endLine int) float64
func RenderText(rows []Row, th Thresholds, w io.Writer) error
expected_types_the_coder_will_implement.note_to_e2e: Write your tests against these signatures as the target API. When the Coder picks them up, the tests will fail to compile — that is Red in the TDD cycle (Rule 1: `not compiling is failing`). Keep the type names stable so the Coder does not need to reinvent the contract.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: crap subcommand is dispatched by the CLI
scenario_to_test_map.assert: After extending cli.Config with `Crap Handler`, cli.Dispatch([]string{"crap", "--coverprofile=cover.out"}, cfg) invokes Config.Crap with args ["--coverprofile=cover.out"].

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: --coverprofile is required / I/O errors exit with code 2 / usage errors exit with code 2
scenario_to_test_map.assert: Call crap.Run(cfg) (or equivalent) with missing coverprofile, nonexistent coverprofile path, and unknown flag. Each returns exit code 2 and writes a diagnostic to Stderr containing the offending token.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: --root defaults to the current working directory / --root selects an explicit analysis root
scenario_to_test_map.assert: Flag parsing yields Root == "." when --root omitted; Root == "./internal/crap" when specified.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: default thresholds are CC 4 and CRAP 30 / threshold flags override defaults
scenario_to_test_map.assert: Default Thresholds{CC:4, CRAP:30}; --threshold-cc=8 --threshold-crap=50 yields Thresholds{CC:8, CRAP:50}.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: Cyclomatic complexity rules (one scenario per rule, 6 total)
scenario_to_test_map.assert: For each source snippet parsed with go/parser, ComplexityOfFunc returns the Gherkin-stated value. Include the zero-branch baseline, two `if`s, for+range, three-case switch, two-case select, and the mixed `&&`/`||` expression.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: anonymous function complexity rolls up into the enclosing top-level function
scenario_to_test_map.assert: A top-level fn with one `if` plus a closure with two `if`s reports a single row with CC == 4 (1 base + 3 ifs). No separate closure row.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: _test.go / vendor/ / hidden dirs / non-Go files excluded
scenario_to_test_map.assert: Set up a fake fs (or testdata dir) with foo.go, foo_test.go, vendor/x/y.go, .git/a.go, .claude/b.go, README.md, pkg/c.go, main.go. Analyze returns rows derived only from foo.go, pkg/c.go, main.go — confirm by set of File fields.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: coverage fraction equals covered stmts / total stmts in overlapping blocks

scenario_to_test_map.assert: Input `"mode: set\npkg/x.go:1.1,2.1 1 1\n"` parses without error and yields exactly one Block; the mode line does not appear as a Block.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: coverage blocks are matched by file path and line range
scenario_to_test_map.assert: With blocks only in 10..15 of pkg/z.go: function A (1..5) gets 0.0; function B (10..15) gets 1.0.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: CRAP score scenario outline (8 example rows)
scenario_to_test_map.assert: For each (cc, cov) pair, the computed CRAP matches within 1e-6 and the returned Band is the Gherkin-stated string. Use a tolerance because 10*0.5*0.5*0.5=1.25 etc. involve floating-point arithmetic.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: text output sorted by CRAP desc then CC desc
scenario_to_test_map.assert: Feed the four-row fixture from the Gherkin; assert the rendered lines mentioning B, C, D, A appear in that order. Also cover a tie on CRAP broken by CC desc.
Output contains each of: `x.go:3`, `F`, the CC literal `5`, the coverage literal `40.0%`, the CRAP literal `12.48`, the band `moderate`.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: violations column lists thresholds that the row exceeds (three sub-cases)
scenario_to_test_map.assert: Row (CC=6, CRAP=12) violates cc only; row (CC=10, CRAP=110) violates both; row (CC=3, CRAP=4) violates neither (empty column).

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: --json emits a JSON array of row objects
scenario_to_test_map.assert: json.Unmarshal into []map[string]any succeeds; every element has keys {file, line, function, cc, coverage, crap, band, violations}; coverage is float in [0,1]; violations is a []string.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: JSON rows ordered by CRAP descending then CC descending
scenario_to_test_map.assert: Same fixture as text sort test; assert array ordering by `crap` field.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: exit code 0 on clean; 1 on any violation
scenario_to_test_map.assert: Clean fixture returns 0. Fixture with CC=5 (threshold 4) returns 1. Fixture with CRAP=30 (threshold 30) returns 1 (>= is a violation). Assert all three cases.

scenario_to_test_map.gherkin: pointing the tool at its own source reports cleanly
scenario_to_test_map.assert: Skip with testing.Short(); otherwise: run `go test -coverprofile=<tmp> ./...` from the repo root via exec.Command, then invoke crap.Run with that coverprofile and --root at the repo root. Expect exit code 0. If the repo currently has a violation (possible during early Coder work), mark this test as t.Skip with a clear message until the Coder resolves it — the Architect will un-skip it at final acceptance.

fixtures_you_should_provide: A few short Go source strings parseable by go/parser/ParseFile representing each CC rule. Keep them literal in the test body; helpers over go/parser are fine but no external libs.

Coverprofile strings embedded directly in tests. Examples: `"mode: set
pkg/x.go:10.2,15.3 4 1
pkg/x.go:16.2,20.3 4 0
"`.

A tiny on-disk fixture tree under internal/crap/testdata/filters/ to exercise the walker (foo.go, foo_test.go, vendor/x/y.go, .git/a.go, README.md, pkg/c.go, main.go). Each .go file can contain a single no-op `package p` declaration — the walker test only cares about inclusion/exclusion, not parsed contents.
do_not: Do NOT implement any production code in internal/crap/ or wire anything into cmd/swarmforge/main.go. That is the Coder's pass.
Do NOT add any module dependency. Stdlib only. If a test seems to need testify-style assertions, write a small local helper instead.

Do NOT invoke `go test` or any real build in a unit test — keep unit tests pure. The self-application acceptance test is the single exception, and must be guarded by testing.Short or marked t.Skip until wiring exists.
Do NOT edit features/swarmforge-crap.feature. Raise any ambiguity back to the Architect.
done_looks_like: Every scenario in features/swarmforge-crap.feature is covered by at least one Go test that fails for the RIGHT reason — missing types / missing implementation, not unrelated compile errors in other packages.
`go test ./internal/crap/... ./internal/acceptance/... ./internal/cli/...` produces compile errors and test failures in the new tests only; all PREVIOUSLY green tests in the repo remain green.

A handoff document `agent_context/handoff-to-coder.json` is written with: the package layout, the expected API signatures, the scenario-to-test map (so the Coder knows which tests enforce which contract), exit-code semantics, and an explicit reminder that `cmd/swarmforge/main.go` and `internal/cli/cli.go` must be extended to wire the new subcommand.
Pane 2 (Coder) is notified via `./swarmforge notify 2 "crap subcommand: red tests ready, handoff at agent_context/handoff-to-coder.json"` once the red tests are in place.
carry_forward: This tool will become the Simplicity Enforcer agent's primary check once the Review/Simplicity agents are added in a subsequent task. Keep exit codes and JSON shape stable — they will be the agent's contract.
